turns.content: Why does this even work this way? I'm trying to implement a versioned segment tree in Rust for an open-source project, but the memory layout feels broken compared to what Java's immutable data structures do. In my current draft using Vec and Box<dyn Any> as the generic node type, the borrow checker is screaming at me because I can't guarantee that every path through the tree holds a valid reference without cloning everything on update.

The frustration comes from treating mutable vectors like immutable objects in Java. In Rust, you need to explicitly manage ownership and lifetime for persistent structures. Instead of wrapping nodes in Box<dyn Any>, consider using a concrete enum with discriminants that distinguish between leaf and internal nodes, combined with an index-based layout or a custom allocator like alloc::vec::Vec where the data is stored contiguously. This avoids the overhead of dynamic dispatch and lets you implement O(log n) updates by only allocating new nodes along the path while reusing old ones elsewhere.
Okay, so I should use an enum for the node types? But how do I actually handle the sharing between versions without copying the whole tree when updating a single index?

Exactly. Use an enum like Node { Leaf(Value), Internal(Vec<Node>) } where each version shares all nodes that aren't on the update path. When you perform an update, you create new versions only for the ancestors of the modified leaf. The rest of the tree is referenced by index rather than cloned. This means your allocator can reuse memory blocks across versions efficiently.

I'm still struggling with the lifetime annotations though. If I define a struct SegmentTree<T> where T: Clone, how do I ensure that when I return a new version it doesn't accidentally borrow from the old one in a way that breaks Rust's rules?

You don't need to clone unless you're modifying shared mutable state. For persistent trees, every update returns a fresh root pointing into an immutable array of nodes. The lifetime is implicit because the old tree lives alongside the new one in memory without overlapping references. Just ensure your helper functions that build paths return owned values and never take &self from the old tree as a reference if they're going to mutate it.
Wait, so I can just pass the old root by value into my update function? But what about the internal nodes that are shared? Do I need to store them in a separate vector or something?

Yes. Store all node data in a single Vec<Node> indexed by version and position. Your root for each version is just an index into that vector. When updating, you generate a new set of indices for the path nodes while keeping old indices intact for everything else. This makes your SegmentTree struct hold only two things: the next version number and the full node array.
This is getting clearer, but I'm worried about performance in competitive programming scenarios where we do many updates quickly. Will this approach beat a standard segment tree?

The constant factor will be higher due to allocation overhead per update, but the asymptotic behavior remains O(log n). For most CP problems with ≤10^5 queries, it's acceptable. If you need raw speed, consider a persistent segment tree built on top of a Fenwick tree or use a dynamic segment tree with pointer-based nodes instead of indexed arrays to reduce fragmentation.
